11 | IMPROVING THE MISSION, GOVERNANCE AND OPERATIONS OF THE EU HERA 
 
1.2 HERA IN THE CONTEXT OF THE EUROPEAN HEALTH UNION 
The establishment of HERA is a key initiative to address problems that emerged in the 
context of the pandemic. Different configurations were considered for HERA, ranging 
from an authority within the Commission to an independent agency10. The inception 
impact assessment published by the Commission in January 2021 still largely 
contemplated the possibility of setting up an independent authority, with different 
options available in terms of ambition (from a purely operational authority to a full end-
to-end institution). This ambition corresponded to President von der Leyen’s 
announcement in her 2020 State of the Union speech, in which she likened the future 
HERA to the US BARDA, a powerful office of the Department of Health and Human 
Services responsible for the procurement and development of medical countermeasures. 
It was originally set up in the aftermath of the 2001 anthrax attacks, and played a key role 
in the quick funding and development of MCMs for Covid-19, including mRNA vaccines11.  
The setup of an independent authority continued to appear to be the preferred policy 
option for the European Commission, at least until the summer of 2021. Yet, eventually 
HERA was established on 16 September 2021 as a service within the Commission, with 
the HERA Incubator being its vanguard12. This institutional setup was met with criticism 
by some stakeholders. While the decision to set up HERA as a Commission service saved 
time and potentially paved the way for quicker decision-making going forward, it also 
framed the policy measure in a way that required only a Council regulation, and no 
involvement of the European Parliament13. Furthermore, numerous EU institutions 
(including the European Parliament, European Court of Auditors, European 
Ombudsman), academics and civil society organisations questioned the transparency and 
accountability of the new office, including the possible involvement of stakeholders in 
HERA’s operations. HERA’s functioning and operation will be reviewed and adapted on 
an annual basis, with a full review due at the latest by 2025. In addition, annual activity 
 
10 As outlined in the 2021 Work Programme of the Commission: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-
content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A52020DC0690. 
11 See https://aspr.hhs.gov/AboutASPR/ProgramOffices/BARDA/Pages/default.aspx; more specifically, 
HERA differs from BARDA in that the latter is not formally an independent agency, although it has ample 
discretion on how to select projects for funding, negotiate with private corporations and use its financial 
resources. 
12 The HERA Incubator is the bio-defence preparedness plan established by the European Commission in 
February 2021. Its goal is to detect and identify the variants of the Covid-19 virus. In 2022, the activities 
of the HERA Incubator are mapped to correspond to Task 1 of HERA on threat assessments and 
intelligence gathering.  
13 European Parliament resolution of 21 October 2021 on EU transparency in the development, purchase 
and distribution of Covid-19 vaccines (2021/2678(RSP)), https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/ 
document/TA-9-2021-0435_EN.html
12 | ANDREA RENDA, CHIARA DEL GIOVANE, HIEN VU, NADINA IACOB 
reports14 and annual management plans15 will be available. Hence, the full 
operationalisation of HERA is still ongoing, and some specific functions are yet to be 
defined or subject to adjustments.  
The Decision establishing HERA sets the following mission: i) strengthening health 
security coordination within the EU during preparedness and crisis response times, and 
bringing together the Member States, industry and relevant stakeholders in a common 
effort; ii) addressing vulnerabilities and strategic dependencies within the EU related to 
the development, production, procurement, stockpiling and distribution of MCMs; and 
iii) contributing to reinforcing global health-emergency preparedness and response 
architecture16. Figure 1, by the European Medicines Agency, shows the allocation of tasks 
among the European Commission (including HERA), EMA and ECDC in the context of the 
emerging European Health Union. As shown in the figure, HERA will be assigned several 
‘peace time’ activities, such as intelligence gathering on health threats, procurement and 
R&D funding. Also included are the mapping of EU infrastructure and the setup of 
dedicated facilities to secure speedy production in an emergency, and emergency 
activities like the coordination of countermeasures. HERA is explicitly meant to operate 
in two modes: a ‘preparedness mode’, intended to serve as the default operation mode; 
and a ‘crisis mode’, for responding to public health threats for a limited period until the 
threat is removed or controlled.  
Some of these activities would, however, require strong and seamless coordination 
within the European Commission (with DG SANTE, for example on joint procurement and 
international actions) and with other institutions (e.g. with EMA’s emergency task force 
and monitoring/management of shortages, and with ECDC’s epidemiological 
surveillance). 
 
14 The Annual Activity Report for 2021 (published on 31.3.2022) is https://ec.europa.eu/info/sites/default/ 
files/annual-activity-report-2021-hera_en_0.pdf  
15 The Management Plan for 2022 (published on 07.6.2022) is https://ec.europa.eu/info/sites/ 
default/files/hera_mp_2022_en.pdf  
16 Commission Decision (EU) on establishing the Health Emergency Preparedness and Response Authority, 
16 September 2021, C(2021) 6712 final.
13 | IMPROVING THE MISSION, GOVERNANCE AND OPERATIONS OF THE EU HERA 
 
Figure 1. The European Health Union’s main institutional landscape 
 
Established as a shared resource of the EU and Member States alike, HERA still features 
developing governance. A current organisation chart and Article 3 of the Commission’s 
Decision establishing HERA set out the governance of this service. It includes a Head of 
HERA, who takes all measures necessary for the functioning of HERA, e.g. prepares the 
multiannual plan and the annual work programme, negotiates procurement and 
contracts for MCMs, and manages financial resources17. A HERA Coordination Committee 
provides political steering on the planning and implementation of HERA’s tasks and 
includes the Vice-President of the Commission in charge of Health, as well as the 
Commissioners for Health, Internal Market, Research and Innovation, and Crisis 
Management. A HERA Board is composed of one high-level representative nominated by 
each Member State and appointed by the Commission. The Commission might invite the 
European Parliament, ECDC, EMA and Emergency Response Coordination Centre and 
other relevant agencies as observers on the Board18. An Advisory Forum comprises 
members from technically competent bodies designated by each Member State, with the 
mandate to provide support to the Board with two subgroups (the Joint Industrial 
Cooperation Forum and the Civil Society Forum)19.  
 
17 The Commission appointed Pierre Delsaux as Head in December 2021. 
18 The Board assists and advises the Commission in the formulation of strategic decisions. It also seeks to 
avoid overlaps with other key structures, e.g. the Health Security Committee, the Vaccine Steering Board 
and relevant committees involved in the management of EU programmes. As of May 2022, the list of 
Board members was not available, except for the head of HERA (Pierre Delsaux) who chairs the Board. 
19 The Joint Industrial Cooperation Forum enables regular and systematic exchanges between Member 
States and industry on preparedness priorities, particularly to ease bottlenecks in the production of 
MCMs. The Civil Society Forum ensures that HERA receives opinions, recommendations and monitoring
14 | ANDREA RENDA, CHIARA DEL GIOVANE, HIEN VU, NADINA IACOB 
In addition, a network of national or regional agencies in Member States will be 
responsible for assessing the availability and accessibility of relevant MCMs in a health 
emergency, feeding into the work of the HERA Board. According to its Work Plan 2022, 
HERA was to finish setting up a network of national or regional BARDA-like agencies or 
entities in Q2 2022.  
1.3 THE OPPORTUNITY COST OF TAKING NO (OR INSUFFICIENT) ACTION IS ENORMOUS 
The fact that several problems have emerged during Covid-19 would not be, by itself, 
justification for far-reaching reforms and enhanced investment at the EU level. Post-hoc 
solutions are not necessarily useful unless events such as Covid-19 are likely to repeat 
themselves in the future. Failing to learn the lessons of Covid-19 is therefore a problem 
only if those lessons can be useful in the future.  
In this respect, there is widespread agreement in the scientific community that events 
such as Ebola, SARS, MERS or Covid-19 are likely to become more frequent and diverse, 
also due to climate change, increased deforestation, environmental degradation, and lack 
of control on animals that are likely to be vectors or hosts of pathogens20. In 2020, a 
report by the Intergovernmental Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services 
estimated that ‘1.7 million currently undiscovered viruses are thought to exist in mammal 
and avian hosts. Of these, 540,000-850,000 could have the ability to infect humans.’ The 
most important reservoirs of pathogens with pandemic potential are mammals (in 
particular bats, rodents, primates) and some birds (especially water birds), as well as 
livestock (e.g. pigs, camels, poultry). The OECD also supported the view that events of 
this magnitude are likely to become more frequent in the future. Among others, the 
Vaccine Alliance (GAVI) argued that five main trends (global travel, urbanisation, climate 
change, increased human-animal contact and health worker shortages) are likely to lead 
to more frequent epidemics and pandemics in the years to come.  
In terms of impact, experts had already warned before Covid-19 that the cost of diseases 
such as pandemic influenza was not far from the estimated global cost of climate change. 
 
by civil society. The Forum will work on issues that would impact on EU citizens. The setup of these two 
forums will finish in Q2 2022 according to HERA’s Work Plan for 2022 (https://ec.europa.eu/health/latest-
updates/european-health-union-hera-call-representatives-civil-society-and-industry-join-its-civil-
society-2022-03-28_en). The Civil Society Forum met for the first time on 28 June 2022 
(https://health.ec.europa.eu/latest-updates/first-meeting-hera-civil-society-forum-2022-06-29_en). 
HERA will hold its first-year conference on 8 December 2022.  
20 In 2021, the UK government even observed that the probability of another massive pandemic by 2030 is 
concrete. See Global Britain in a Competitive Age: The Integrated Review of Security, Defence, 
Development and Foreign Policy, published on 16 March 2021, https://www.gov.uk/government/ 
publications/global-britain-in-a-competitive-age-the-integrated-review-of-security-defence-
development-and-foreign-policy/global-britain-in-a-competitive-age-the-integrated-review-of-security-
defence-development-and-foreign-policy.
15 | IMPROVING THE MISSION, GOVERNANCE AND OPERATIONS OF THE EU HERA 
 
That cost is also further nurtured by a cycle of ‘attention and neglect’, as confirmed during 
the Ebola epidemic in West Africa in 2014 as well as the H1N1 and SARS outbreaks.  
Overall, the following conclusions are broadly accepted by the scientific community: 
• there is a significant probability that several other serious cross-border threats to 
health will affect Europe between now and 2030 (and beyond); 
• there is a reasonable prospect of another large-scale, high-impact pandemic in the 
next few decades; 
• outbreaks of communicable diseases are becoming more frequent and deadlier, also 
due to the interplay between climate change, environmental degradation and loss of 
biodiversity, all of which might lead to ideal conditions for the emergence or re-
emergence of infectious diseases of zoonotic origin; 
• accordingly, the magnitude, likelihood, impact and diversity of cross-border health 
threats will increase over the coming years, making the currently defined problem 
likely to worsen over time.  
As a result, failing to take action to remedy some of the failures observed in EU multi-
level health security governance would represent a growing source of risk for the EU. 
Moreover, there is little doubt, in the academic literature or in the policy community, that 
investment in preparedness and effective response can significantly mitigate the impact 
of cross-border health threats. Already, researchers are starting to show that swifter 
reaction to Covid-19 could have saved as much as 5 % of global GDP. For France, Gollier 
(2021) estimates that a doubling of the speed of vaccination achieved in March 2021 
could have reduced deaths by a third for the whole year. As reported by Castillo et al. 
(2021), the IMF estimated global GDP losses from Covid-19 of USD 12 trillion during 2020-
2021, an average monthly GDP loss of USD 500 billion. However, more comprehensive 
harm estimates – including education and health losses – are considerably larger. For 
example, Cutler et al. (2021) estimated comprehensive harm in the US to be over five 
times the projected GDP loss. Castillo et al. (2021) calculate that if the world had managed 
to put in place a vaccine production infrastructure capable of distributing 1.2 billion 
monthly doses of vaccines as of January 2021, the avoided damage to the global economy 
would have reached almost USD 5 trillion.  
A report by the Global Preparedness and Monitoring Board shows that the return on 
investment for preparedness is extremely high: it would take 500 years to spend as much 
on preparedness as the world is currently losing due to Covid-19. In 2015, the Boston 
Consulting Group quantified the cost and time benefits of a large and diversified 
investment ‘portfolio’ of the emergency preparedness actions taken by UNICEF and the 
World Food Programme in 2014. It found that 75 % of these investments saved significant
16 | ANDREA RENDA, CHIARA DEL GIOVANE, HIEN VU, NADINA IACOB 
time and/or costs in the event of an emergency. Notably, 93% of these programmes 
saved time; 64 % saved both costs and time. Among the actions with the highest return 
on investment were pre-positioning of emergency supplies, large infrastructure projects 
and training21.  
Investing effectively in enhanced preparedness and response can mitigate socioeconomic 
and behavioural impacts in different ways: (i) reducing the likelihood that an event 
occurs; (ii) improving the ability to detect outbreaks early; (iii) identifying and analysing 
transmission mechanisms at the level of local communities; (iv) deploying and procuring 
efficient medical countermeasures; and (v) supporting resilience-oriented policymaking 
at all levels of government.  
It is possible to distinguish between two main categories of activities that pertain to the 
health emergency preparedness and response domain.  
• A first set of activities occur ‘between emergencies’, i.e. before a threat has 
materialised (‘peace time’). These include several important activities, such as  
i) 
foresight and threat landscape mapping, R&D development and support, 
development of preparedness plans, simulation and stress tests, definition of 
arrangements for rapid prototyping and development of medical 
countermeasures; procurement and stockpiling of medicines and protective 
equipment;  
ii) 
policy advice to institutions (e.g. the European Commission) on how to 
preserve and improve resilience through policymaking and spending 
programmes;  
iii) 
the setup of a robust data exchange system with national and local 
authorities; provision of training services within and outside the institution, 
training of local health professionals and awareness-raising activities for the 
population and local authorities; use of new technologies and artificial 
intelligence for prediction or early identification; etc.  
Importantly, some of these activities do not affect the likelihood that a threat will 
materialise, but are aimed at more efficient and effective detection, containment and 
mitigation of outbreaks. On the other hand, a subset of these activities can also have 
a preventative impact, which implies that not only the consequences, but also the 
likelihood of an outbreak is reduced. Good examples include the provision of policy 
 
21 In the broader area of disaster risk prevention, Hawley et al. (2012); Shreve and Kelman (2014); and 
Mechler (2015) find solid evidence of high return on investment for preparedness investments. According 
to a study, every euro invested in public health gives an average return of EUR 14 to the economy 
(Masters et al. 2017). While this estimate covers a wide range of public health interventions, more specific 
studies on preparedness have shown even greater return on investment.
17 | IMPROVING THE MISSION, GOVERNANCE AND OPERATIONS OF THE EU HERA 
advice on One Health approaches, which reduces possible diseases of zoonotic origin, 
and action aimed at improving the sanitary conditions of buildings, which reduces the 
probability of contact with pathogens for inhabitants. 
•
A second category of activities includes those crisis management tasks that must be 
performed when a threat materialises (‘emergency time’). While the previous set of
activities occurs on a regular basis, these only occur in the case of an outbreak, and
are therefore directly related to the likelihood, spread and size of outbreaks in the
future (see below). They include:
i)
early detection and warning activities;
ii) the redirection and management of R&D funding, the coordination of response
activities on the ground, the negotiation of agreements with institutions and
businesses (e.g. pharmaceutical companies) for the swift and inclusive
distribution of medical countermeasures, including vaccines and antibody tests;
iii) procurement of medical countermeasures and protective equipment, including
masks, ventilators and respirators, and oxygen supplies;
iv) rapid policy advice to institutions on the adoption on non-pharmaceutical
interventions;
v) participation in the definition of additional policy measures aimed at mitigating
the likelihood of disease spread (e.g. approaches to tracing-and-tracing, including
digital contact-tracing);
vi) guidance to institutions on measures aimed at the containment of indirect health
problems, such as the exclusion of specific categories (e.g. undocumented
migrants), and the lack of care for patients with pre-existing morbidities (e.g.
cancer patients)22;
vii) overall execution of the pandemic preparedness plan, including coordination with
national health authorities and other EU authorities, such as EMA, ECDC and the
Civil Protection Mechanism.
22 94 % drop in diagnosed cancers during Covid-19.
18 | ANDREA RENDA, CHIARA DEL GIOVANE, HIEN VU, NADINA IACOB 
1.4 THE EMERGING EU GLOBAL HEALTH STRATEGY 
Investing in resilience by ensuring more effective coordination during and between 
emergencies in Europe is, however, likely to be insufficient to significantly mitigate future 
risks. Resilience against future health emergencies is a textbook example of a global 
public good, and as such present important externalities and collective action problems, 
calling for enhanced coordination. In the case of Covid-19, despite widespread 
acknowledgement of the fact that ‘no one is safe until everyone is safe’, the global 
community showed shocking examples of lack of solidarity, as reported by the Lancet 
Commission 2022. The global response to Covid-19 failed almost at every level of 
government. The need to reform global health-security governance was immediately felt, 
as it became clear that most countries (with some exceptions, mostly in Asia and Oceania) 
were far from prepared for the pandemic. Moreover, selfishness and inward-looking 
policies were trumping existing multistakeholder partnerships such as the Access to 
Covid-19 Tools Accelerator (ACT-A) and Covid-19 Vaccines Global Access (COVAX), 
leading to slow and patchy delivery of medical countermeasures in low- and middle-
income countries. It became also clear that the commercial interests of pharmaceutical 
companies were being prioritised over the speedy delivery of existing solutions where 
fast action was needed. In addition, the ‘binding’ nature of the International Health 
Regulation was suffering from a critical lack of enforcement.  
Today, the prospect of a new pandemic accord and the creation of new financial 
instruments and funds to boost preparedness for future pandemics are at the centre of 
a hectic discussion, which we extensively cover and discuss in a companion report. What 
is important to recall, for the purposes of this report, is that any action by HERA to boost 
resilience in Europe would be incomplete if HERA were not deeply involved in promoting 
enhanced, reliable cooperation with similar authorities in other countries. The same 
would happen if a framework for coordinating preparedness and response at different 
levels of government were not agreed upon at the WHO level, as well as in other 
plurilateral forums (e.g. the G7, and the G20). Furthermore, the need to boost 
international cooperation calls for innovative solutions in terms of R&D cooperation, joint 
foresight, multistakeholder partnerships, and technology and knowledge transfer to build 
production capacity in less developed countries.  
Against this backdrop, the European Commission has on several occasions declared its 
intention to step up its role as an actor in global health governance, expanding previous 
strategies such as the one contained in the 2010 Commission communication and the 
Council Conclusions on ‘The EU Role in Global Health’. At the G7 meeting of health and 
development ministers on 19 May 2022, Commissioners Stella Kyriakides and Jutta 
Urpilainen announced the launch of a process to develop a new EU Global Health
19 | IMPROVING THE MISSION, GOVERNANCE AND OPERATIONS OF THE EU HERA 
 
Strategy. Immediately after this announcement, in the context of the May 2021 Global 
Health Summit, President von der Leyen committed to the relaunch of the Global Health 
Policy Forum, paving the way for a public consultation on an EU strategic framework for 
global health, which ran until 19 September 2022.  
Adopted by the Commission on 30 November 2022, the EU Global Health Strategy 
focuses on three priorities, namely i) delivery of better health and well-being of people, 
ii) strengthening of the health systems and advancing universal health coverage; and iii) 
prevention and responses to health threats (including pandemics) using the One Health 
approach. The strategy features the external dimension of the European Health Union 
and a key component of the EU Global Gateway, a strategy to build infrastructure in 
developing countries that aims to mobilise EUR 300 billion between public and private 
funds. 
The Strategy highlights the overall role of HERA in improving preparedness and responses 
in the field of medical countermeasures. What this will entail in practice, however, is still 
unclear, as the competence for international dialogue seems to have remained in the 
hands of DG SANTE. Accordingly, the contribution of HERA to global health security may 
appear to be ‘indirect’: by strengthening preparedness and response within the EU, HERA 
will also contribute to global resilience, without operating as an actor in global health-
security governance.  
Still, in order for the EU to fully implement its ambitious new Global Health Strategy, a 
more proactive role for HERA will be needed. For example, HERA could seek enhanced 
cooperation with the many ad hoc institutions set up by third countries with the specific 
mandate of strengthening emergency preparedness and response. These include BARDA 
(overseen by the Office of the Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response); the 
UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA, an executive agency of the Department of Health and 
Social Care), the Korean Disease Control and Prevention Agency (KDCA), and the Japanese 
Infectious Diseases Surveillance Centre. Such cooperation may focus on areas like 
foresight on future health emergencies for better situational awareness, R&D 
cooperation (also to avoid duplicative investments) and the coordination of response at 
the regional and global level in emergencies. In our companion report on the emerging 
architecture of global health-security governance, we provide more detail on the role 
that HERA-like institutions may play in the context of revamped global cooperation for 
health resilience. The annex in this report presents a comparison between the resources 
and mandates of institutions similar to HERA in the US, UK, Switzerland, Japan and Korea.
20 | ANDREA RENDA, CHIARA DEL GIOVANE, HIEN VU, NADINA IACOB 
2. THE PRESENT AND FUTURE OF HERA: AN ANALYSIS OF SIX ASPECTS 
In the aftermath of the establishment of HERA, we at the Centre for European Policy 
Studies (CEPS) decided to set up an Expert Working Group on alternative configurations 
for the institutional design of HERA (hereinafter, the ‘Expert Group’) to discuss options 
for the design of HERA and potential future improvements23. The Expert Group 
comprised 39 experts, including 9 from academia, 9 from civil s

AAAC, November 2013 Helou-3 
 
IRAS: First mid-to-far-IR All-Sky Survey  
  IRAS was the first IR all-sky survey, at 12, 25, 
60 and 100µm: Si and Ge photo-conductors 
  Collaboration between US, Netherlands, UK, 
1983 
Main data products: 
  Point Source Catalog ~1 Jy sensitivity 
  Faint Source Catalog, a few times deeper 
  All-Sky Image Atlas at 4΄ resolution 
  On-demand co-added survey data 
  Compact sources 
  Resolution enhancement
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-4 
 
ISO: First Pointed IR Observatory in Space 
  Four versatile instruments, 2 imagers, 2 
spectrometers, covering 3-240µm: Si, Ge 
photoconductors (1995-98)  
  ESA mission with minor NASA, ISAS participation
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-5 
 
  Three instruments, imaging at {3.6, 4.5, 6.8, 8}, 
{24, 70 and 160} µm, 1 spectrometer 5-38µm, 
SED mode 60-120µm: Si, Ge photoconductors 
  NASA mission, August 2003 – May 2009 cold 
  Warm mission since 2009: 3.6, 4.5 µm imaging 
Spitzer: the NASA IR Great Observatory
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-6 
 
  Three instruments: imaging at {70, 100, 160}, 
{250, 350 and 500} µm; spectroscopy: grating 
[55-210]µm, FTS [194-672]µm, Heterodyne 
[157-625]µm; bolometers, Ge photoconductors, 
SIS mixers; 3.5m primary at ambient T 
  ESA mission with significant NASA contributions, 
May 2009 – April 2013 cold 
[OIII] 88 µm 
z = 3.04 
Herschel: Cornerstone FIR/Submm Observatory
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-7 
 
  Two instruments, nine bands: HFI {857, 545, 
353,  217, 143, 100} GHz, LFI {70, 44 and 30} 
GHz; bolometers cooled to 100mK, HEMT 
  ESA mission with significant NASA contributions, 
May 2009 – Jan 2012 cold; to Oct 2013 LFI only 
Planck: State-of-the-Art CMB+Astrophysics Survey

AAAC, November 2013 Helou-8 
 
ISO: Deal and Outcome 
  ISO was an ESA-only mission, with some US individual participation 
as co-investigators on instrument teams and on the ISO Science 
Team, and some “scientiﬁc associates” 
  Individual access to Guaranteed Time 
  Late-breaking agreement: DSN time for 30min/orbit (~30min/day) of 
Guaranteed Time, plus access to Open Time competitions (no quota) 
  ISAS funded additional operations shift, also for 30min/orbit of GT 
  NASA & ISAS named 1 representative each to the ISO Science Team 
  Two or three US-based scientists were invited to join the TAC 
  NASA competed its GT independently, selecting 4 Key Projects 
  In Open Time, the US community was allocated ~30% of the time 
(PI), and participated on many more selected proposals 
  Net result: US community was responsible for ~25% of ISO time 
  NASA Astrophysics investment was all in Data Analysis funding and 
community support at IPAC 
  Data quality issues, slightly ameliorated by IPAC help (late arrival)
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-9 
 
Spitzer: Deal and Outcome 
  Spitzer is a NASA Great Observatory. 
  Spitzer TAC and review committees included non-U.S. scientists as 
a matter of course, and all calls were open worldwide 
  European scientists were responsible for ~20% of Open Time (PI) on 
cryo-Spitzer, and participated on many more selected proposals 
  For Cycle 10 (Oct 2013) 15% of successful PI’s were foreign-based 
 
 
  NB: ISO-SIRTF, XMM-AXAF debates were similar to debate on 
Euclid-AFTA/WFIRST 
  Lesson 1: NASA participation in “similar” ESA missions does not kill 
prospective NASA-led  missions 
  Lesson 2: XMM, ISO prepared US community for Chandra, Spitzer, and 
provided experience for design of mission and operations
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-10 
 
Herschel: Deal and Outcome (1) 
  Herschel is an ESA Cornerstone Mission (~B$ class) with signiﬁcant 
NASA contributions (10-15% of mission cost up to launch) 
  Community advocacy for Herschel-like mission was a Transatlantic 
movement, and ESA moved on it ﬁrst 
  Instrument proposals to ESA had US co-I’s and hardware components 
  NASA primary H/W participation was in enabling detector 
technologies for 2 instruments 
  Bolometers and ampliﬁers assembly for SPIRE, plus expertise 
  SIS mixers and other components for HIFI, plus expertise 
  Instrument Team participation results in access to GT 
  Two US-based mission scientists and one optical system scientist 
were selected in open competition, with additional access to GT
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-11 
 
Herschel: Deal and Outcome (2) 
  Aside from the above, ESA and NASA exchanged LoA: 
  NASA provides Science Operations expertise and software (Spitzer 
heritage), shares any s/w or documentation developed at NHSC/IPAC 
  NASA provides resident astronomer at Herschel Science Center in Spain 
  ESA provides “appropriate US scientist representation on HOTAC” 
  ESA provides NHSC with full access, as “integral part of the Herschel 
Science Ground System” 
  NB: ESA-NASA reciprocally open proposal calls; no quotas on Herschel 
  Net result: Open Time calls on Herschel have resulted in U.S. PI’s 
carrying about half the Open Time, in addition to GT participation 
  Additional participation by US co-I on ~35% of Open Time 
  A third of all Key Projects had U.S. PI’s and all had U.S. participation 
  Data quality issues were addressed quickly, and NHSC has much 
more insight, ability to help (compared to ISO) 
  U.S. activity on publications so far reﬂects proposal success rate
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-12 
 
Planck: Deal and Outcome (1) 
  Planck is an ESA Mid-Sized Mission with signiﬁcant NASA 
contributions (10-15% of mission cost up to launch) 
  Community advocacy for Planck-like mission was a Transatlantic 
movement, and ESA moved on it ﬁrst 
  Instrument proposals to ESA had US co-I’s and hardware components 
  NASA primary H/W participation was in enabling new technologies 
  Spider-web bolometers and ampliﬁers for HFI, including polarization-
sensitive bolometers, plus expertise 
  HEMT radio ampliﬁers for LFI, plus expertise 
  Hydrogen sorption coolers to get down from passive (~50K) to ~20K 
  One (2) US member on the Science Team, ~80 US Planck scientists 
  Fully integrated team, access to data, software, discussions, analysis 
and results 
  E.g. Planck Editorial Board co-chaired by U.S. scientist
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-13 
 
Planck: Deal and Outcome (2) 
  Agreements between NASA and CNES and ASI 
  NASA provides engineering support for delivered H/W 
  NASA provides support for mission design & planning, data analysis 
  US Planck scientists account for 20-25% of data analysis activity 
  Lead many activities and papers, and participate in essentially all 
  Planck papers are mostly “Planck Collaboration, authors-alphabetical” 
  DoE-NASA agreement provides main simulations capability for Planck 
(supercomputing at NERSC) 
  First Planck data release, “Early-Release Compact Source Catalogue 
(ERCSC)”, was produced in US 
  First look at all-sky catalog at λ>300μm, ~104 sources, very fast release 
  The Planck Archive is available at both ESA (ESAC) and NASA (IPAC) 
  NASA Archive has unique tools for enhanced data usability, especially by 
non-CMB community (local detector time-lines for sources, local map 
construction)
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-14 
 
Lessons Learned: Big Picture 
  NASA and ESA can both fund, build and operate major missions 
  Euclid is happening, as will other major missions on both sides 
  ISO, XMM did not kill SIRTF, AXAF, and WMAP did not kill Planck 
  Euclid by itself will not kill AFTAWFIRST, nor will NASA buying into Euclid 
  Research communities function largely as global entities, will push 
missions towards complementarity, will optimize across boundaries 
  Principle 1 captures this. Need to recognize critical role of community 
  The U.S. has great strengths in leading-edge technologies, but more 
especially in human resources, and institutional traditions of research 
support by agencies and universities 
  With access & support, U.S. community will get its share of the science 
  U.S. contributions, properly targeted, will yield rich science dividends 
for U.S. community and enrich the science globally 
  Ultimately, a richer science return from the mission is good for everyone 
  Similarity of science goals worldwide is an opportunity: partnerships are 
very valuable stepping stones between US-led missions, for community 
and for project-level planning
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-15 
 
Lessons Learned: Elements of Partnership (1) 
  Proven formula: combine grass-roots science collaboration, special 
or unique hardware contributions, and a NASA Science Center 
(community support based on participation in science data system) 
  Agencies’ role: create a high-level framework appropriate for the speciﬁc 
mission and supportive of grass-roots collaboration 
  Good relations at working level are crucial, so high-level framework 
should encourage participation, and let working relations develop: 
  Among scientists: build a science community for the mission 
  Among instrument/payload builders: optimize interfaces locally 
  Among Science Centers: learn by doing, add value for all users 
  Agency-level framework should recognize community needs, and 
stress reciprocity not detailed deal-making 
  Scientists then focus on science rather than worry interpretation of rules 
  Framework is needed very early: leave room for ﬂexibility, evolution 
  Critical Mass of participation is important: 
  Thin presence makes for difﬁcult interactions; 10% share seems to be a 
reasonable threshold
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-16 
 
Lessons Learned: Elements of Partnership (2) 
  An integrated mission community sets stage for “level playing ﬁeld” 
and is best guarantee of “fair science return” 
  No quotas on science exploitation helps U.S. and global science return 
  Principle 4 captures this 
  However, capturing science return in a global competitive 
environment requires proper support for the home team 
  U.S. agencies have diverse approaches for this support, but 
ultimately two aspects are needed to “level the playing ﬁeld” 
  Funding people to analyze data and publish results 
  Shared structure and services to support common needs robustly, 
efﬁciently e.g. supercomputing, observing facility or mission science 
center
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-17 
 
Agency Support: Role of NASA Science Centers 
  NASA Science Centers were created to enable broadest access  
  National Academy of Sciences advised that major telescopes “not be 
used by only a few astronomers, but [that] a large part of the community 
must be closely involved with the instrument over a long period of 
time.” (Institutional Arrangements for the Space Telescope, 1976) 
  Efﬁcacy recently validated by NAS in “Portals to the Universe” report 
  The “NASA Science Centers have transformed the conduct of much of 
astronomical research and set in place a new paradigm for the use of all 
large astronomical facilities”, remedying “what had become an insular 
culture for accessing space astronomy data.” (2007) 
  Science Centers (CXC, IPAC, STScI) have created a competitive 
edge for U.S. in science exploitation, one of few remaining 
  Advantage of national scale 
  Principles 2 & 3 will enhance importance of Science Centers: Value of 
Open Access to data (increasingly Big Data) and facilities (increasingly 
sophisticated) is limited by ability of individuals to exploit that access
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-18 
 
Further Thoughts on the “Principles” 
  Principle 4: “openly advertised criteria that are equally applied” 
  This point should apply more broadly, especially for #2 (Open Data) and 
#3 (Open Facilities) 
  Should be articulated early 
  Principle 2 correctly addresses Open Data, “standard data products 
made public in a timely and usable manner” 
  The ability to extract more advanced information is left in the competitive 
sphere. This ability is critical in the era of Tera-scale and Peta-scale 
surveys 
  Providing support to enhance this ability is critical, through targeted 
community funding and through targeted Science Center activities  
  e.g. NHSC virtual machines, supercomputing resources
AAAC, November 2013 Helou-19 
 
Footnote: Where I Learned my Lessons 
  Member or leader of many observing projects, science investigations 
  Served as NASA representative on ISO Science Team, led one of the 
U.S. Key Projects on ISO (resulted in ~20 refereed papers) 
  Co-I on original proposals that grew into Herschel and Planck 
  Active Planck “Core Team Scientist”, member of Editorial Board 
  Advised NASA on Herschel participation agreement  
  Frequent attendee of Herschel Science Team meetings as observer 
 
  As IPAC Director 
  Responsible for NASA Herschel Science Center 
  Responsible for U.S. Planck Data Center (Data availability to U.S. team, 
ERCSC generation, U.S. Planck Archive construction)

AAAC, November 2013 Helou-1 
 
Lessons Learned !
from Past and Current ESA-NASA Partnerships 
Astronomy & Astrophysics Advisory Committee 
November 2013 
 
George Helou 
IPAC, Caltech

Prepared by:
Giovanni Bignami, Peter Cargill, Bernard
Schutz and Catherine Turon on behalf of
the Science advisory structure of ESA, and
by the Executive of the Science
Directorate, supported by Nigel Calder
Published by:
ESA Publications Division, ESTEC, PO Box
299, 2200 AG Noordwijk,The Netherlands

Vatican (Stanza della Segnatura,Palazzi Pontifici) perhaps
depicts the embodiment of Astronomy.
(Copyright Photo SCALA,Florence)
Replacing the original astronomical globe is Mars viewed by
the High Resolution Stereo Camera (ESA/DLR/FU Berlin,

merging into an image (G. Hasinger,Astrophysikalisches
Institute,Potsdam) of X-ray sources in the Lockman Hole made
using the Newton X-ray Observatory spacecraft.
Chapter divider
